Familiarization to
Ultra Thermic
Cutting Sub Sea
Broco BR-22 Plus · Oxy-Arc Cutting Operations Field Manual
Produced By
F. Schrijver & M. Jeffery
Primary Source
Broco BR-22 Plus Operator Manual
Industry Standard
IMCA D 003 Rev.2 · Sep 2023
Document Type
Familiarization Manual
Additional Reference
ANSI Z49.1:2005 · ADCI Standards
Power Requirement
DC Only — 150A Min · 400A Max
⛔ Critical Safety Notice

Underwater cutting and welding equipment is potentially dangerous and can result in serious injury or death. This equipment must be used by trained commercial divers only, in strict accordance with these instructions, using surface supplied breathing air and hard-wire communications. Never use SCUBA. Never use AC power. Never operate without a topside tender and standby diver.

Source Documents Broco Inc. — BR-22 Plus Underwater Cutting Torch Operator Instructions (09-26-11) · Broco Inc. — Underwater Cutting Products Operating Instructions · IMCA D 003 Rev. 2 — Guidelines for Oxy-Arc Cutting (September 2023) · ANSI Z49.1:2005 — Safety in Welding and Cutting · ADCI Consensus Standards for Commercial Diving · OSHA 29 CFR 1910 · US Navy NAVSEA 0929-LP-000-8010
01
What Is Ultrathermic Cutting?
Introduction to oxy-arc cutting technology and the Broco system
Broco BR-22 Plus underwater oxy-arc cutting torch with oxygen valve lever and power lead.
Commercial diver carrying out subsea ultrathermic cutting on steelwork using the BR-22 Plus torch.

Oxygen-arc (oxy-arc) cutting is a thermal cutting process in which metal is severed by means of the chemical reaction of oxygen with the base metal at elevated temperatures. An electric arc brings the metal to its kindling temperature, after which a high-velocity jet of pure oxygen directed through a tubular cutting electrode causes the metal to oxidise and be blown away. The electrode tip — exposed to both extreme heat and oxidation — is consumed in the process and must be replaced by the diver at regular intervals.

Ultrathermic cutting rods (such as the Broco Underwater Ultrathermic rod) are a specific type of exothermic electrode. Once ignited by the arc, the rod generates temperatures in excess of 5,538°C at the tip through an exothermic chemical reaction. After ignition, the rod will continue to burn as long as the oxygen flow is maintained — even with the electrical power switched off. This is the key operational characteristic that distinguishes ultrathermic rods from steel tubular electrodes, which require continuous power.

💡
Key Distinction: Ultrathermic vs. Steel Tubular

With ultrathermic (exothermic) rods, once ignited under oxygen flow, the diver calls "make it cold" — the electrical power is isolated — and cutting continues. The rod burns solely on the exothermic reaction sustained by oxygen. The only way to extinguish the rod is to release the oxygen lever. Steel tubular rods require continuous power throughout the cut.

Typical Applications

🔩 Structure Removal

Removal of redundant steelwork, damaged tubulars, caissons, and access hole preparation

🔗 Seized Fasteners

Cutting of seized bolts subject to removal or replacement where mechanical methods have failed

🏗️ Abandonment

Major decommissioning and abandonment operations on offshore installations and pipelines

🚧 Access Preparation

Removal of obstructing steelwork to provide access for installation or inspection activities

⚓ Marine Growth

Cutting through rope, wood, marine growth, concrete and rock — non-conductive materials with the power off

🔧 Emergency Response

Emergency cutting during salvage operations, entrapment scenarios and unplanned field tasks

⚠️
Oxy-Arc Is Always the Last Resort

Due to the risk levels from oxygen, high-voltage electricity, and the underwater environment, all other cutting options must be reviewed prior to committing to oxy-arc. IMCA D 003 classifies oxy-arc cutting as a 'safety critical' activity. Flow charts for method selection are provided in Section 03. Cold cutting methods (mechanical, hydraulic) eliminate oxygen/fire risk entirely.

Standards and Regulatory Basis

This manual is based on and traceable to the following standards. All operations must comply with applicable local regulations where these are more stringent.

  • IMCA D 003 Rev.2 (September 2023) — Guidelines for Oxy-Arc Cutting [Primary industry guidance]
  • Broco Inc. BR-22 Plus Operator Manual (09-26-11) — Equipment-specific operating instructions
  • ANSI Z49.1:2005 — Safety in Welding and Cutting
  • ADCI Consensus Standards for Commercial Diving
  • OSHA 29 CFR 1910 — Safety and Health Standards
  • IMCA D 045 — Code of Practice for the Safe Use of Electricity Under Water
  • US Navy NAVSEA 0929-LP-000-8010 — Underwater Cutting and Welding Manual
02
How the Process Works
The oxy-arc cutting cycle from ignition to rod change
Principle of ultrathermic cutting: the arc ignites the rod, the oxygen jet sustains the exothermic burn, and oxidised metal is blown clear from the cut.

The Broco ultrathermic cutting system combines two energy sources: a DC electrical arc for initial ignition, and a sustained exothermic chemical reaction — the "ultrathermic burn" — maintained entirely by high-flow pure oxygen once ignited. Understanding this dual-phase operation is fundamental to safe and effective cutting.

Phase 1 — Arc Ignition

  1. 1
    Oxygen Pre-Purge

    The diver presses and holds the oxygen lever for a minimum of 4 seconds with the rod tip at the cut position. This purges explosive gases from the rod and establishes oxygen flow before any electrical current is applied.

  2. 2
    Arc Strike

    With oxygen still flowing, the diver calls "make it hot". The topside tender closes the safety knife switch. The diver draws the rod tip across the work surface to strike an electrical arc. Temperatures at the tip immediately exceed 5,000°C.

  3. 3
    Exothermic Ignition

    The arc brings the rod tip and surrounding metal to kindling temperature. The exothermic materials within the rod ignite in the pure oxygen stream. The rod is now burning independently.

Phase 2 — Ultrathermic Burn (Power Off)

  1. 4
    Isolate Electrical Power

    On rod ignition, the diver calls "make it cold". The tender opens the safety knife switch. The rod continues to burn at >5,538°C sustained only by the oxygen flow and the internal exothermic reaction. No further electrical power is required.

  2. 5
    Metal Oxidation

    The iron oxides in the rod and the base metal undergo a rapid exothermic oxidation reaction. Metal is not merely melted — it is oxidised and blown away by the high-velocity oxygen jet. This is why the process cuts through materials that cannot be conventionally welded or melted.

  3. 6
    Maintain Contact

    Unlike arc welding, do NOT hold an arc gap. Keep the burning rod tip in direct contact with the work at all times. Hold the rod "like a pool cue" with the free hand approximately 100 mm from the tip for a stable cut. The angle of the rod to the work: 30 to 90 degrees depending on material thickness.

  4. 7
    Rod Consumed — Extinguish

    When the rod burns to within 75 mm of the collet nut, the diver lifts the rod off the work and releases the oxygen lever. The rod immediately extinguishes. The tender confirms current is off before the diver loosens the collet nut to remove the rod stub.

🔴
Critical: Rod Burns Without Power

Broco ultrathermic cutting rods will continue to burn as long as oxygen flow is maintained, even with the power completely off. The ONLY way to extinguish the rod is to release the oxygen lever. Switching off the welding machine will not stop combustion.

Burn Time and Performance Parameters

Parameter Value / Specification Notes
Tip Temperature> 5,538°CAt ignition and during burn
Rod burn time (exothermic)45 – 55 secondsPer rod under normal conditions
Rod burn time (steel tubular)~60 secondsContinuous power required
Max burn time — normal cutting< 60 secondsIf >60s, inadequate O₂ flow — see troubleshoot
O₂ purity required≥ 99.5%Below 99.5% causes erratic burning. Each 1% reduction ≈ 25% efficiency loss
Base O₂ delivery pressure6.2 bar[g] over ambientAt the cutting rod tip — increase with depth
Amperage for ignition150A at electrodeHigher for steel tubular (up to 400A)
Current typeDC onlyAC will electrocute the diver
PolarityElectrode NEGATIVE (−)Ground/earth POSITIVE (+)
03
Hazards & Danger Zones
Comprehensive hazard identification — IMCA D 003 §3.3 compliant
☠️
Oxy-Arc Cutting Is a High-Risk Activity

Underwater explosions from oxy-arc cutting have occurred in industry and some have been fatal. They occur from the simultaneous presence of explosive gases (oxygen and hydrogen), ignition heat from the electrode, and gas build-up in enclosed spaces. Every cut requires a thorough risk assessment prior to commencement.

Major Risk Categories

💥 Gas / Explosion
CRITICAL
  • Explosive gas mixtures (O₂ + H₂) ignited by cutting spark
  • Gas entrapment in enclosed spaces, voids, sealed tubulars
  • Cutting into pressurised chambers or pipelines
  • Oxygen/hydrogen trapped in mud below mudline
  • Steam explosion from rapid water heating in enclosed space
⚡ Electrocution
CRITICAL
  • AC power — immediately fatal in water
  • Diver positioned between electrode and ground (diver becomes the circuit)
  • Splash zone — partial immersion with live circuit
  • Contact with live rod to helmet/dress/hose/cylinder
  • Damaged or improperly taped cable connections
🔥 Burns & Flashback
HIGH
  • Flash arrestor failure allowing hot gas backflow to torch
  • Oxygen pop — pressure wave strong enough to move diver's hands
  • Molten slag spray on diver, life support equipment, umbilical
  • Rod burning back into torch (collet nut not secured)
  • Hands within 100 mm of electrode tip
🏗️ Mechanical / Physical
MEDIUM-HIGH
  • Falling or moving cut sections injuring diver
  • Collapse of weakened or cut structural members
  • Hose failure under pressure — line whip injury
  • Jamming of cut during operation
  • Entanglement in umbilical, hose, or cut material
🌊 Environmental / Operational
MEDIUM
  • Limited visibility — misidentification of cut location
  • Current — loss of position during active cutting
  • Restricted access — inability to maintain correct body position
  • Skill fade — infrequent use of technique
  • DP system hydroacoustic interference from waste gas
🩺 Life Support Hazards
HIGH
  • Oxygen contamination of diving bell atmosphere (saturation)
  • Hot gas plume contaminating diver's breathing supply
  • Cutting torch contacting life support umbilical
  • SCUBA use — prohibited, no comms, shock risk
  • Modified life support equipment from manufacturer specs

IMCA Risk Assessment Guidewords

The following guidewords from IMCA D 003 §3.3 must be addressed in every risk assessment for oxy-arc cutting operations:

GuidewordConsideration Required
Annular CavitiesAssess whether annular cavities are blocked and unable to free-vent escaping gases. Blocked venting creates explosion potential.
ArcingInspect all electrical cables for abrasion before deployment. Visual examination required surface and subsea.
BlowbackSpontaneous explosions at the cut point from hydrogen formation or oxygen ignition. Good technique and diver positioning reduce risk.
Client ApprovalAny client prohibition on oxy-arc must be established early. PTW must be in place before operations commence.
Cutting GuideConsider use of cutting guides to define the working area and assist accurate cuts, especially in poor visibility.
Diving Bell PositionGas plumes must not contaminate bell atmosphere. Use pneumofathometer to assess plume direction relative to bell.
DP SystemWaste gas can interfere with hydroacoustic position reference. Umbilical management must address proximity to thrusters.
Enclosed SpacePARAMOUNT: Initially cut a vent hole to allow gas escape before any main cut. Consider cold-cutting the vent hole first.
Flash BackFlash arresters protect against oxygen ignition propagating into equipment. Must be on scheduled maintenance programme.
Hydrocarbon ResidueCheck for hydrocarbon residues within enclosed spaces or under structures. Hydrocarbons ignite spontaneously in oxygen.
Pressure DifferentialPressure differential when cutting into enclosed spaces, especially pipelines. Drill and equalise before cutting.
Platform Risers/CaissonsManage potential for unconsumed oxygen to accumulate within risers/caissons. Coordinate with platform management.
Securing LoadRig and support cut sections before severance to prevent the cut piece dropping on the diver.
Vent HoleExit hole higher than the planned cut position to allow unconsumed and waste gas to escape. Must be adequate size for full venting.
04
Gas Explosion Risks
Underwater gas accumulation — the primary fatal hazard in oxy-arc cutting
Typical explosion mechanism: an ignited rod breaches a sealed or poorly vented member containing flammable gas or an oxygen-enriched mixture, leading to overpressure and blast discharge.
💥
Explosive Force Increases With Depth

Oxygen entrapment at the worksite creates explosive potential. This explosive force is proportionally greater at increased depth due to higher partial pressures. At 30m depth, the explosive energy of a given gas volume is approximately 4× that at surface.

Sources of Explosive Gas Accumulation

🔵 Unconsumed Oxygen

High-flow oxygen through the cutting rod — any excess not consumed in the exothermic reaction escapes as free oxygen. In confined spaces, pockets of pure oxygen accumulate. Even a small ignition source causes violent combustion of any nearby material.

🟡 Hydrogen Generation

The cutting process — particularly when burning through wet or coated steel — generates hydrogen through electrolytic action and chemical reactions. Hydrogen mixed with oxygen produces a highly explosive mixture (minimum explosive concentration: 4% H₂ in air).

🟠 Trapped Gas Pockets

Air-filled tubulars, caissons, sealed compartments, and structural boxes all present trapped gas risks. When the cutting rod penetrates the seal, the expanding hot gases and new oxygen can cause an immediate explosive reaction.

🔴 Mud-Trapped Gas

Oxygen and hydrogen can become trapped in seabed sediment and marine mud. Before cutting below the mudline, the area must be jet-lifted, airlifted, or pumped clear from behind the cut to eliminate this hazard.

Control Measures — Gas Explosion Prevention

  1. A
    Vent Holes — Mandatory for Enclosed Spaces

    Before any main cut on an enclosed member, drill or cut a vent hole above and behind the planned cut position. The vent must be sized to allow continuous gas escape. Consider cold-cutting the vent hole first, then oxy-arc for the main cut.

  2. B
    Inert Gas Flooding

    Flood work areas with an inert gas such as argon or nitrogen before cutting. This displaces oxygen/hydrogen mixtures and eliminates the explosive atmosphere.

  3. C
    Pressure Equalisation

    When cutting where a pressure differential exists (either blowing out or sucking in), drill a small hole first and wait for pressure to fully equalise before proceeding. Pressure differentials amplify the explosion risk and can cause sudden violent gas release.

  4. D
    Mud Clearance

    Before cutting below the mudline, jet and airlift or pump mud clear from behind the cut zone. Never cut into sediment without clearing it first.

  5. E
    Check Behind the Cut

    Always investigate what material, gas, or fluid may be present behind the material being cut. If gas presence is suspected, treat as high risk until confirmed otherwise.

  6. F
    Concrete Venting

    When melting through concrete, ensure the entry hole is large enough to allow venting of all gases generated in and above the work area during the melt.

⚠️
Cutting Into Enclosed Spaces — Pre-Cut Vent Hole Protocol

When cutting into any enclosed space (tubular, caisson, box section, pipeline), the initial entry vent must be cut first, higher than the planned main cut position, and must be confirmed open and flowing before the main cut begins. The choice of whether to use oxy-arc or cold-cutting for the vent hole itself must be made in the risk assessment — if explosive gas contamination is suspected, the vent must be cold-cut.

05
Electrical Hazards
Polarity, grounding, shock prevention and electrical circuit control
⚡
AC Power Will Kill the Diver

Using alternating current (AC) for underwater cutting can electrocute the diver. NEVER use AC power for underwater cutting operations. Use ONLY direct current (DC) from a known, verified power source. The power source polarity must be checked and confirmed before any diver enters the water.

Correct Polarity — Critical Configuration

The electrical circuit must be configured with the correct polarity. Incorrect polarity will prevent arc ignition and indicates a dangerous configuration fault that must be resolved before diving.

−
NEGATIVE
Cutting Torch / Electrode
→ DC FLOW →
+
POSITIVE
Ground / Earth Clamp

Source: IMCA D 003 Rev.2 §4.1 — Electrical polarity. Electrode (consumable rod) must be connected to the negative (−) terminal. Earth/ground G-clamp must be connected to the positive (+) terminal.

Polarity Test Procedure (Unknown/Unmarked Generator)

  1. 1
    Disconnect Power at Source

    With the welding generator dead, connect the ground and welding leads to the generator terminals.

  2. 2
    Prepare Test Setup

    Attach a small steel plate to the ground cable. Insert a cutting rod in the torch. Immerse both the plate and rod tip in a container of salt water approximately 50 mm apart.

  3. 3
    Energise — Observe Bubble Location

    Supervisor energises the torch ("make it hot").

  4. 4A
    ✓ Correct — Bubbles from Rod Tip

    A heavy stream of bubbles rises from the cutting rod tip = straight polarity = DC electrode NEGATIVE (−). This is correct. Ground clamp is POSITIVE (+). Proceed.

  5. 4B
    ✕ Incorrect — Bubbles from Plate

    If bubbles appear from the plate: switch off, swap the lead connections to opposite terminals, re-test. Label the terminals for future reference.

Electrical Safety Rules

  • Knife switch rating: Single pole single throw (SPST) or double pole single throw (DPST), rated minimum 400A DC, unfused. IMCA requires 600A minimum rating. Double-pole preferred as it breaks both conductors simultaneously.
  • Knife switch position: Must be within reach of the topside tender at all times during diving operations. Tender must be able to open it immediately on diver command or emergency.
  • Knife switch control: Only activated on direct command from the diver. Never energise without diver instruction.
  • Rubber gloves: Electrical protection from rubber gloves is mandatory for all oxy-arc cutting operations. Surgical under-gloves can be worn under rubber gloves for additional protection.
  • Splash zone: Oxy-arc cutting within the splash zone must be avoided — diver exposure at the air/water interface with live circuit is extremely high risk.
  • Diver positioning: Never allow the diver to position himself between the ground connection and the cutting rod. The diver must always face the ground connection. Becoming part of the electrical circuit is potentially fatal.
  • Cable connections: All cable-to-cable connections must be wrapped with rubber strips and UL-approved electrical insulating tape extending at least 100 mm up each side of the connection.
  • Separate battery: Never use the vessel's primary battery as the power source for rod ignition — it can damage the battery and render the vessel inoperable.
  • Verify with ammeter: Do NOT rely on dial indicators on the welding power source. Use a clamp-on or inline ammeter to verify actual current output.
⚠️
Changing Rods — Open the Knife Switch First

To prevent electrical shock to the diver and damage to life support equipment, always ensure the safety knife switch is open (circuit broken) before changing cutting rods. Confirm this verbally with the tender before loosening the collet nut.

06
Equipment Required
Complete equipment list with specifications — Broco and IMCA requirements combined
High-flow underwater oxygen regulator for ultrathermic cutting service.
Broco underwater oxygen regulator with dual gauges for cylinder and delivery pressure monitoring.

Primary Equipment — Mandatory

#ItemSpecification RequiredSource
1Cutting TorchBroco BR-22 Plus — designed for diver comfort, maximum O₂ flow, ease of operation and maintenanceBroco
2Cutting RodsBroco Underwater Ultrathermic cutting rods — 6 mm or 9.5 mm diameter (see Section 08 for selection)Broco
3Oxygen RegulatorBroco HVR-4401 high volume two-stage regulator, capable of 1.98 m³/min at 6.2 bar. Must incorporate flashback arrester and pressure relief valve on downstream LP sideBroco/IMCA
4Safety Knife SwitchSPST or DPST, rated 400A DC minimum (IMCA: 600A), unfused. Insulated or shielded to protect operator from arcing.Broco/IMCA
5Ground ClampC-type (G-clamp) rated for underwater use. Attach to POSITIVE (+) terminal of DC supply.Broco
6DC Welding Power Source150–200A, 100% duty cycle. DC output verified. Separate from vessel primary battery. Output verified with clamp ammeter.Broco
7Welding Cables — PowerSize #1/0 minimum (50mm²) for torch power lead. #2/0 (70mm²) or #3/0 (90mm²) for longer runs. See Table 2, Section 11.Broco/IMCA
8Welding Cable — GroundMatching cable from DC positive terminal to ground G-clamp. Minimum #1/0.Broco
9Oxygen SupplyGas only (no liquid oxygen). 99.5% purity minimum. HP oxygen cylinders or manifold quad. Sufficient volume for full operation plus contingency.Broco/IMCA
10Oxygen Supply Hose9.5 mm internal diameter minimum for torch supply. Rated for oxygen service. Taped to torch power cable at 600 mm intervals. Must not be crimped or restricted.Broco
11Diving Umbilical (Oxy-Arc)Combined umbilical incorporating: O₂ supply hose, electrical power cable, earth/ground cable. D-rings spliced at 15m intervals for weight support. Treated with same respect as diver umbilical.IMCA

Diver Personal Equipment

🥽 Eye Protection

Approved welding lens attached to diving helmet. Minimum shades: #4 (muddy water), #6 (average), #8+ (clear water). Battery power: #6. Welding machine: #10.

🧤 Rubber Gloves

Linesman-type rubber electrical insulating gloves. Mandatory at all times during cutting. In good condition, no cuts or punctures.

🧤 Surgical Under-Gloves

Worn under rubber gloves. Additional electric shock protection layer. Recommended by IMCA D 003 §4.1.

🤿 Diving Dress

Full wet or dry suit. Must insulate diver's body. No exposed skin in electrical circuit zone. Hard-wire communication helmet mandatory.

🔩 Spare Collets & Washers

At least one spare set of each collet size to be used. Attached to cutting torch assembly prior to deployment. Collet washer (silicone rubber, 9.5 mm) is a consumable item.

📋 Consumable Rods

Sufficient quantity prepared for the full task plus contingency. Inspect each rod — do NOT use damaged rods. Tape must be removed before surface practice use.

🚫
Prohibited: Do Not Use a "Burning Bar"

Do NOT use a burning bar for underwater cutting operations. A burning bar is any underwater cutting rod operating with oxygen as a fuel source whose diameter is greater than 9.5 mm OR length is longer than 914 mm. Burning bars cause excessive gas build-up and oxygen explosion. They are prohibited.

07
BR-22 Plus Torch Components
Complete parts list, function of each component, and maintenance-critical items
BR-22 Plus cutaway showing internal oxygen path, flashback protection features, insulation, and valve arrangement.
Assembled BR-22 Plus torch with insulated body, oxygen lever, hose connection, and electrical cable lug.
Simplified torch layout added for familiarisation — shows the main working areas the diver and tender need to recognise.

Improved Features (BR-22 Plus vs Previous Model)

Higher O₂ Flow Rate

Increased oxygen flow compared to previous BR-22 model, improving cutting efficiency at all depths.

Bronze Sintered Slag Arrestor

Large-area sintered bronze element. Increased flow, withstands more heat and sparks than previous design.

Improved Collet & Washer

Redesigned collet provides tighter rod hold. Washer removed from direct oxygen contact — seals on rod outside, not tip. Extended washer life.

Ceramic Rod End Insulator

Eliminates electrical insulator cut-through. Withstands extremely high temperatures — non-combustible.

Backflow Preventer Valve

Stops high-temperature gases from passing back through torch head in the event of a blowback event.

Fail-Safe Control Valve

Redesigned to shut off O₂ flow even with complete O-ring seal failure. Lever allows manual return if valve stem sticks — critical diver control in adverse conditions.

Teflon-Lined Hose

Fiberglass-reinforced, Teflon-lined hose. Completely non-conductive. Combustion resistant in pure oxygen. Withstands extreme operating temperatures.

Flexible Insulator Gasket

Creates tight seal between handle halves. Reduces electrolysis and more effectively insulates operator's hand from electrical current.
