Factory-Assembled Non-Serviceable Units

🔴
Do Not Disassemble These Items — Replace As Complete Units

The following assemblies are factory-assembled, soldered, or hydraulic-pressed and must be replaced as complete single units. Any attempt to disassemble voids the warranty and releases Broco from all liability:

  • Flash arrestor assembly (flash arrestor nut + ceramic washer) — factory soldered
  • Coupler hose assembly (coupler hose + fittings + shrink sleeve) — hydraulic press assembled
  • Check valve assembly — sold as single replacement part only
08
Cutting Rods & Selection
Rod types, dimensions, material applications, and performance data
Broco ultrathermic cutting rods in storage tube. Common sizes shown are 6 mm and 9.5 mm diameter rods.
Rod detail and metric size selection guide for common subsea cutting tasks.
⚠️
Only Use Broco Rods with Broco Torch

The use of underwater cutting rods from other manufacturers can cause flashbacks or internal burning and injury to the diver. Always use Broco Underwater ultrathermic cutting rods with the BR-22 Plus torch. The collet and washer dimensions are matched to Broco rod diameters.

Rod Types Compared

FeatureUltrathermic (Exothermic) RodSteel Tubular Electrode
Requires continuous powerNo — burns on O₂ after ignitionYes — continuous DC required
Burn time per rod45–55 seconds~60 seconds
Can cut non-conductive materialsYes — concrete, rock, rope, coralNo
Can cut without powerYes — once ignitedNo
Required amperage~150A for ignition onlyUp to 400A continuous
O₂ consumptionHigher volume requiredLower O₂ volume
Material thickness capabilityAll thicknessesUp to 50mm
Kerf widthWiderNarrow, clean
Electrode holder wearLower (lower amperage)Higher (continuous high amps)
Operational simplicitySimple techniqueSimple technique

Interactive Rod Selection Tool

Select Material & Thickness — Get Recommended Rod

Recommendation
Recommended Rod—
Rod Diameter—
O₂ Pressure (Surface)—
Est. Cut per Rod—
Power Setting—
Special Technique—
Notes—

Rule of Thumb — Cut Length Per Rod

Rod DiameterPlate ThicknessRange of Cut Per Rod
6 mm6 mm500–635 mm
6 mm12 mm250–380 mm
9.5 mm12 mm300–510 mm
9.5 mm25 mm230–355 mm
9.5 mm38 mm200–305 mm

Source: Broco Inc. Operating Instructions. Note: US Navy NAVSEA 0929-LP-000-8010 gives estimates at the high end of these ranges for 9.5 mm rods.

Rod Insertion — Correct Orientation

🔄
Insert Correct End Into Torch

Always insert the recessed end (with internal wires visible) of the cutting rod into the collet — the untaped, recessed end. Insert fully until it bottoms on the collet washer, then tighten the collet nut. The taped/painted end faces out as the cutting tip. Inserting a rod backwards will prevent ignition and may damage the torch.

Battery-Powered Operations

A 12V or 24V DC vehicle or boat battery may replace a welding machine as the ignition power source when using 9.5 mm Broco rods.

⚠️
Battery Operations — Depth Limit

Use of a battery power source is not recommended for cutting at depths below 60 fsw (18m). At greater depths, insufficient ignition power may result in failed ignition attempts and accumulated oxygen buildup. A safety knife switch is recommended even with battery power. To preserve battery life, the tender must disconnect the battery immediately on the diver's command once the rod is ignited.

09
Pre-Use Inspection & Pressure Test
Mandatory checks before every deployment — do not skip any item

O-Ring and Seal Inspection

Before any operation, the following O-rings and seals must be inspected. Use oxygen-safe silicone grease on all O-rings except the collet washer.

🛢️
Oxygen-Compatible Lubricants ONLY

Do NOT use any oil or petroleum-based grease on any internal or external torch parts. Use oxygen-safe silicone grease only. Do NOT apply any lubricant to the reddish-brown collet washer. Do NOT use standard Teflon tape — use oxygen-safe green Teflon tape or oxygen-safe pipe joint compound on fitting threads.

Seal / O-Ring LocationPart No.Check ForAction if Damaged
Collet O-Ring (inside collet)Collet assemblyCracks, nicks, cuts, distortionReplace before use. Lightly grease with O₂-safe silicone.
Collet Washer (reddish-brown, back of collet)BRW38Cuts, deformation, deteriorationReplace. DO NOT lubricate this washer.
Handle-to-Collet Nut Seal (large O-ring on outside of handle)BRRN123Cracking, cuts, nicksReplace. Apply O₂-safe silicone grease.
Valve Stem Teflon O-RingsBRRT007 (×2)Wear, distortionReplace. Note: Valve designed to seal even with O-ring failure.
Neoprene O-RingBRRN014Hardening, crackingReplace.

Backflow Preventer (Non-Return Valve) Test

  1. 1
    Disconnect from oxygen supply

    Ensure torch is completely disconnected and not pressurised.

  2. 2
    Depress the torch lever

    Hold the oxygen lever in the open (depressed) position.

  3. 3
    Blow into the torch head

    Blow into the front (head/tip end) of the torch — opposite to normal oxygen flow direction.

  4. 4
    Verify: No flow through the torch

    There should be absolutely no gas flow through the torch. Any detectable flow indicates a failed non-return valve — the torch must not be used until repaired.

BR-22 Plus Pressure Test Procedure

Perform this test prior to every use. Test pressure is 9.0 bar[g] — higher than normal operating pressure (6.2 bar[g]) specifically for leak detection.

  1. 1
    Connect to oxygen supply

    Connect torch to its oxygen supply hose and regulator.

  2. 2
    Set test pressure: 9.0 bar[g]

    Normal operating pressure is 6.2 bar[g]; 9.0 bar[g] is for testing purposes only. Do not exceed this pressure during testing.

  3. 3
    Seal the torch head

    Use ONE of three methods: (a) insert a sealed-off cutting rod or solid brass test slug; (b) remove collet, place a solid rubber welding collet washer inside, replace collet with rod/stub and tighten normally. Do NOT tighten collet nut without a rod in place — it will bend the collet tabs.

  4. 4
    Submerge and pressurise

    Place entire torch in a bucket of water. Pressurise the torch and depress the lever to ensure the full torch is pressurised.

  5. 5
    Hold for 30 seconds — observe for bubbles

    Keep lever depressed underwater for at least 30 seconds. Observe for bubbles. Initial trapped air may produce a few brief bubbles — these will stop after a few seconds. A steady flow of bubbles = leak = repair required before use.

  6. 6
    Safe removal of test plug

    Remove torch from water. Disconnect from oxygen supply. Depress lever to relieve pressure. Point head away from persons/property. Remove collet nut slowly while holding test plug — the plug is under pressure and WILL eject if the collet nut is released without first depressurising.

  7. 7
    Repair and retest if required

    Any leak found requires repair and a full re-test before the torch may be used.

10
Assembly & Setup
Surface preparation — assembling the complete cutting system before deployment

Complete all steps in sequence. Do not proceed to the next step until the current step is confirmed. All components must be clean, oil-free, and inspected. Reference: IMCA D 003 Appendix 2 — Surface Preparation Checklist.

Added setup flow image for quick topside familiarisation before reading the detailed step sequence.
  1. 1
    Initial Component Inspection

    Inspect hose and cables — no visible cuts, cracking, splitting. Cable lug connectors securely attached, no excessive corrosion. Check torch for gaps in handle seam. Verify flash arrestor, collet washer, and collet are in place and in good condition.

  2. 2
    Purge Oxygen Supply

    Open the oxygen cylinder valve briefly to purge it, then close. Attach the oxygen regulator. Open and close the cylinder valve again to purge the regulator. Attach the oxygen supply hose to the regulator outlet. Ensure supply valve, regulator, hose and torch are clean and free of any grease, oil or particles.

  3. 3
    Attach Torch to Oxygen Supply

    Connect torch to oxygen hose. Purge regulator and hose with oxygen to clear any obstructions. Tape oxygen hose to power cable at 600 mm intervals along the full run.

  4. 4
    Set Oxygen Delivery Pressure

    Set regulator delivery pressure to 6.2 bar[g] over ambient bottom pressure. Adjust for working depth using Table 1 (Section 11) plus hose length correction. Regulator must incorporate flashback arrester and pressure relief valve on LP downstream side.

  5. 5
    Leak Check with Soap Suds

    Purge torch. Check all fittings, connections and the torch for leaks using soap suds. No bubbles = no leaks. Repair any leaks found before proceeding.

  6. 6
    Electrical Circuit Setup

    Attach safety knife switch to torch power lead. Attach other end to welding power source negative (−) terminal. Ensure knife switch is within reach of tender. Confirm tender understands — knife switch is activated ONLY on direct command from the diver.

  7. 7
    Insulate All Connections

    Wrap all cable-to-cable connections with rubber strips and UL-approved electrical insulating tape. Tape must extend at least 100 mm on both sides of every connection. Make watertight.

  8. 8
    Ground Cable

    Attach ground cable to welding power source positive (+) terminal. Attach other end to ground G-clamp. Confirm ground cable is not connected to the structure yet — this is done by the diver subsea.

  9. 9
    Set Amperage — 150A at Rod Tip

    With safety knife switch open, start the welding power source. Set output for 150 amps maximum at the rod tip, compensating for cable length and size using Table 2 (Section 11). Verify with clamp-on or inline ammeter — do NOT rely on the welding machine dial.

  10. 10
    Polarity Verification

    Immerse rod tip and ground clamp in a bucket of salt water approximately 50 mm apart. Close knife switch. Bubbles should rise from the rod tip. If bubbles come from the clamp — reverse polarity and retest. Label terminals.

  11. 11
    Function Test on Deck

    Insert a test rod. Confirm oxygen flow (150 mm / 300 mm jet from rod tip horizontally). Perform hot work on deck if required by PTW. Confirm all systems functional before diver entry.

  12. 12
    Isolate Before Deployment

    Isolate the electrical supply (open knife switch). Leave the oxygen line charged at the correct working depth pressure. Isolate at the quad valve. Oxygen line stays charged to prevent water ingress into the hose.

Diver Equipment Check

  • Welding power source confirmed as DC output only
  • Diver in full wet or dry suit — no exposed skin
  • Rubber electrical insulating (linesman) gloves — in good condition, no cuts or holes
  • Welding lens shade appropriate for water visibility attached to helmet faceplate
  • Standby diver ready and briefed for emergency response
  • Topside tender in position at knife switch — cannot leave position during active cutting
  • Communications confirmed — hard-wire only. No SCUBA under any circumstances.
11
Settings & Interactive Calculators
Depth pressure tables, amperage tables, and live calculators

Interactive Pressure Calculator

Oxygen Regulator Gauge Pressure Calculator

Metric conversion of the Broco formula: Gauge pressure (bar[g]) = 6.21 + (0.101 × depth in metres) + (0.0226 × hose length in metres). Enter working depth and hose run to get the required topside regulator gauge setting in bar.

O₂ Gauge Pressure
—
Depth Correction
—
Hose Correction
—
Required Amperage
—

Metric form used here: Gauge pressure (bar[g]) = 6.21 + (0.101 × depth_m) + (0.0226 × hose_m). Amperage is calculated from the Broco table using cable length converted from metres, with +2A for each additional 15m above 152m.

Table 1 — Oxygen Delivery Pressure for Depth

Pressure gauge settings below account for water depth only. Add hose friction correction separately: +0.226 bar per 10m of hose. Hover rows to highlight.

Depth (m) Gauge Setting (bar) Equivalent (kPa[g])
107.2720
127.4740
188.0800
248.6860
309.2920
379.9990
4310.51050
4911.11110
5511.71170
6112.31230
6712.91290
7313.51350
7914.21420
8514.81480

Source: Broco Inc. Underwater Cutting Products Operating Instructions, Table 1. Values shown here are the working metric equivalents for field use.

Calculation method: Add 0.101 bar for every metre of working depth to the base 6.21 bar[g] at the tip. Add 0.0226 bar for every metre of hose for friction losses.

Table 2 — Amperage Setting for Cable Length and Size

Settings compensate for resistance losses in the cable run. For cable lengths over 152m, add 2A per additional 15m to the 152m value.

Cable Length (m) #1/0 (50mm²)#2/0 (70mm²)#3/0 (90mm²)
46155152150
61157154152
76159156154
91161158156
107163160158
122165162160
137167164162
152169166164

Source: Broco Inc. Table 2. Metric cable lengths shown here are the converted working values. For cable runs exceeding 152m: add 2A per additional 15m.

12
Deployment Procedure
Full offshore JSA-compliant deployment sequence — IMCA D 003 §4.5.1
📋
Pre-Deployment Requirements

Before any deployment: JSA completed and relayed to all relevant parties. Client approval and PTW in place. Dive supervisor has reviewed the project risk assessment. Fire watcher designated. Area cordoned off. Equipment function tested on deck.

Subsea Site Assessment (Before Cutting Begins)

  1. 1
    Identify and Mark Cut Location

    Diver correctly identifies and marks the cutting location. Second diver or ROV validates location independently before any cutting commences. This prevents cutting the wrong component.

  2. 2
    Gas Entrapment Assessment

    Diver and ROV assess the full worksite for potential gas entrapment in cavities, structural members, or overhead areas. Use pneumofathometer to assess gas discharge direction relative to diving bell position. Adjust bell position to keep gas plumes clear of bell atmosphere.

  3. 3
    Identify and Confirm Vent Hole Positions

    Diver identifies position of all required vent holes and marks them. Second diver or ROV validates. Dive supervisor AND client's representative must confirm agreement before proceeding. Determine whether vent holes will be cut with oxy-arc or cold-cut.

  4. 4
    Rig Cut Section (if required)

    If the intended cut section may move or fall once severed, rig and secure it before cutting. Assess the risk of the cut piece dropping onto the diver, umbilical, or adjacent assets.

  5. 5
    Deploy Oxy-Arc Cutting Umbilical

    Deploy with DC power supply isolated. O₂ line remains charged at working depth pressure. D-rings at 15m intervals attached to running shackles on downline (midwater) or chained to tugger wire/clump weight (seabed). Ensure no sharp edges on overboarding sheave. Oxygen hose must not become crimped.

  6. 6
    Diver Manoeuvres Umbilical to Worksite

    Secure sufficient length at worksite to conduct full cutting operations without having to reposition. Deck crew secures umbilical on deck — confirm oxygen hose is not crimped anywhere in the run.

  7. 7
    Connect Ground Clamp

    Diver identifies ground connection point. Remove paint/corrosion to expose bare metal. Connect to redundant steelwork or component being cut — NOT to existing infrastructure. Diver must confirm he is NOT between the earth and the cutting torch. Connect G-clamp.

  8. 8
    Install Protection and Cutting Guide

    Install protective material over adjacent steelwork vulnerable to accidental rod contact or molten slag. Install cutting guide if necessary to define the cut path — especially useful in poor visibility.

Diver Self-Positioning Rules

✓ Correct Positioning
  • Always face the ground connection
  • Position body offset — never directly in line with the cut
  • Position such that no molten slag can land on diver or umbilical
  • Hold rod 100 mm from tip with free hand (pool cue grip)
  • Ensure full comfortable range of cut without repositioning
  • Maintain rubber gloves at all times
✕ Never Do This
  • Never turn your back to the ground connection
  • Never position body between ground and cutting rod
  • Never allow hands closer than 100 mm to rod tip
  • Never allow umbilical to be in potential slag path
  • Never work in the splash zone with live circuit
  • Never contact rod to helmet, hose, cylinder, or any life support equipment
13
Cutting Operations
Complete step-by-step subsea cutting procedure — from rod insertion to completion
Maintain a stable body position, keep the rod in contact with the work, keep the umbilical out of the slag path, and work at the correct angle for the section thickness.

Rod Loading and Oxygen Test

  1. 1
    De-Isolate Oxygen

    Dive supervisor requests deck crew to de-isolate the oxygen supply at the regulator. Confirm LP gauge reads 6.21 bar / 6.2 bar above working depth pressure.

  2. 2
    Insert Cutting Rod

    Loosen collet nut 1/2 turn. Insert the recessed end (internal wires visible) into the collet until it bottoms on the collet washer. Tighten collet nut securely to lock rod.

  3. 3
    Test Oxygen Flow

    Squeeze the torch lever. Hold torch horizontally — a minimum 150 mm / 300 mm oxygen jet should issue from the rod tip. Less than this indicates inadequate flow — investigate before cutting. See Troubleshooting (Section 17).

  4. 4
    Check for Collet Leaks

    With oxygen flowing, check for bubbles escaping at the collet nut. If bubbles escape: remove and reseat the rod on the collet washer. If bubbles continue — stop, do not use torch until repaired. A loose connection can cause arcing and flashback.

Ignition and Cutting Sequence

  1. 5
    Position and Pre-Purge (4 Seconds)

    Place rod tip against the work at the start of the cut. Squeeze and hold the oxygen lever for at least 4 seconds — this purges explosive gases from the rod and establishes full oxygen flow before any electrical power is applied.

  2. 6
    Call for Power — "Make It Hot"

    With oxygen still flowing, diver calls "make it hot". Tender closes safety knife switch. Diver draws the rod tip across the work to strike the arc.

  3. 7
    Confirm Ignition — "Make It Cold"

    Once the arc is struck and the rod ignites, diver calls "make it cold". Tender opens knife switch — power isolated. The rod continues to burn on the exothermic reaction. Confirm ignition by visual/sound — the rod should be glowing and burning audibly.

  4. 8
    Begin Cut — Pull Motion

    Pull (drag) the burning rod along the cut line. Rod angle: 30 to 90 degrees to the work depending on metal thickness. Maintain pressure to keep the rod tip against the work. Keep the rod tip in the molten puddle at all times. Move slowly at first — ensure full penetration before increasing speed.

  5. 9
    Technique Notes

    Do NOT try to hold an arc gap. Keep the burning rod in direct metal-to-metal contact. Hold "like a pool cue" — free hand 100 mm from rod tip for stable guidance. Move free hand back as rod consumes. Back-spray and increased cutting noise indicate insufficient penetration — slow down.

  6. 10
    Rod Near Stub — Call Power Off

    When rod has burned to within approximately 75 mm of the collet nut, call "make it cold". Lift rod from work. Release oxygen lever — rod extinguishes immediately.

  7. 11
    Tap Test for Power-Off Confirmation

    Tap the rod stub on the cut position — this visually confirms to the diver and topside that the power circuit is dead (no arc/spark). Loosen collet nut 1/2 turn and remove rod stub. Tender must confirm current is off before removal.

  8. 12
    Check Cut and Insert New Rod

    Check cut for bridges (incomplete cuts) — pass a hacksaw blade or 6mm welding rod along the cut path to confirm it is fully severed. Insert new cutting rod and repeat the process until the cut is complete.

  9. 13
    Completion — Shutdown Sequence

    Dive supervisor requests DC generator be turned off. Dive supervisor requests oxygen supply isolated at regulator and turned off at supply quad. Recover subsea umbilical in reverse of deployment — diver clears equipment from worksite. Deck crew vents residual pressurised oxygen from umbilical on recovery.

✅
Visual Indicators of Good Cutting

Correct cutting produces: bright glowing arc zone, clean kerf with smooth edges, consistent burn rate (rod consumed at steady rate within 45–55 seconds), and a visible melt pool at the rod tip. Oxygen jet of 6"+ horizontally from rod tip before insertion confirms adequate O₂ flow.

14
Cutting Techniques by Material
Material-specific methods, rod selection, and special procedures

Cutting Mild Steel

6 mm Rod — Thin Steel
  • Use for steel up to 12 mm thick for maximum economy
  • Leaves a narrow kerf — preferable for precise or fine cuts
  • Continuous power recommended — maintaining ignition is difficult without it at this diameter
  • 500–635 mm cut on 6 mm plate; 250–380 mm cut on 12 mm plate per rod
9.5 mm Rod — Thicker Steel
  • Use for steel 12 mm and thicker
  • Better production in bad visibility or thick sections
  • 300–510 mm cut on 12 mm plate; 230–355 mm cut on 25 mm plate per rod
  • For very thick material: use saw-and-wedge technique

Saw-and-Wedge Technique (Thick Steel)

For steel sections many tens of millimetres thick where the full-width kerf cannot be maintained in a single pass: employ a saw-and-wedge cutting motion, analogous to chopping wood. Make a series of partial cuts progressively deepening toward full penetration. This prevents rod waste from cutting into already-melted material.

Cutting Stainless Steel, Cast Iron & Non-Ferrous Metals

ℹ️
The Ultrathermic Rod Will Melt All These Materials

The exothermic reaction generates sufficient heat to melt stainless steel, cast iron, and most non-ferrous metals. The technique is modified compared to mild steel cutting.

  • Bury the tip firmly in the work — stainless and cast iron require greater penetration pressure than mild steel
  • Saw-and-wedge cutting may be necessary for thick sections
  • Maximum cutting efficiency: burn a series of holes through the piece prior to cutting across it — the holes become the kerf and reduce resistance
  • Stainless steel does not oxidise as readily as mild steel — greater power may be needed to maintain the melt pool

Cutting Non-Ferrous Metals (Aluminium, Bronze, Copper)

⚠️
Increased O₂ Pressure for Thick Non-Ferrous

For non-ferrous metals over 75 mm in thickness: increase oxygen delivery pressure to 7.6 bar[g] over ambient pressure (vs standard 6.2 bar[g]). This compensates for the higher thermal mass and lower oxidation rate.

  • Create a molten puddle first by dwelling on the surface
  • Dip the rod tip slightly below the surface of the molten metal
  • Periodically push the cutting rod deeper into the metal — this washes out and blows slag away
  • Non-ferrous metals have high thermal conductivity — the melt pool dissipates heat quickly; maintain aggressive rod-to-work contact

Cutting (Melting) Concrete and Rock

⚠️
9.5 mm Rod Only — Striker Plate Required

Always use the 9.5 mm diameter rod for concrete and rock. A striker plate (copper or steel plate connected to the ground cable) is required to ignite the rod, since concrete is non-conductive.

  1. 1
    Strike Arc on Striker Plate

    Light the rod by striking the tip against the copper/steel striker plate attached to the ground cable with oxygen flowing and power on.

  2. 2
    Keep Oxygen Flowing — Call Power Off

    Keep the oxygen lever depressed. Raise the rod from the plate. Call for current to be switched off. Rod continues burning on exothermic reaction.

  3. 3
    Press into Concrete

    Press the burning rod tip into the concrete/rock to create a molten pool. The extreme heat melts the silica and calcium compounds.

  4. 4
    In/Out Motion to Vent

    Periodically move the rod in, out, and around to enlarge the hole, vent gases, and blow molten material clear. Ensure the entry hole is large enough to vent all gases generated in and above the work area.

Cutting Rope and Wood

  • Striker plate required — same technique as concrete (non-conductive material)
  • Light rod against striker plate. Keep oxygen flowing. Call current off. Bring rod to work.
  • Apply firm pressure to the tip to keep maximum heat in direct contact with the material
  • In ship work, the rod may be angled or bent up to 90 degrees to prevent damage to hull, shaft, or stern tube
  • On wire rope: use the 6 mm diameter rod for finer cut control. With the 6 mm rod, continuous metal-to-metal contact is necessary to maintain rod ignition.

Very Thick / Multi-Layer Cuts

  • Use 9.5 mm rod for all cuts above 12 mm plate
  • Drill a series of access holes through the piece prior to cutting across — the holes form the initial kerf and reduce resistance
  • Saw-and-wedge technique: work progressively like splitting wood, alternating between cuts to open the kerf
  • For non-ferrous materials over 75 mm: increase O₂ to 7.6 bar[g] over ambient
  • Accept higher rod consumption on thick sections — do not try to rush
  • Check penetration frequently — insufficient penetration is indicated by back-spray and increased cutting noise
15
Interactive Checklists
Tap items to mark as complete — tracks your progress in real time

Surface Preparation Checklist — IMCA D 003 Appendix 2

0%
  • 1. Risk Assessment Reviewed — Dive supervisor confirms he has reviewed the project risk assessment and completed an offshore job risk assessment (JRA)
  • 2. Client Approval & PTW — Client approval for oxy-arc burning is in place. Relevant permits to work (PTW) are confirmed in place before operations commence
  • 3. Deck Area Clear of Hydrocarbons — Deployment area for oxy-arc cutting system assessed and clear of hydrocarbon residues and combustible materials in event of O₂ leak or electrical arcing
  • 4. Fire Fighting Equipment — Suitable firefighting equipment available on deck and adjacent to the oxygen gas quad
  • 5. Fire Watcher Designated — Qualified fire watcher designated to monitor topside area throughout oxy-arc cutting operations
  • 6. Area Cordoned Off — Area cordoned off to prevent non-essential personnel from being within the vicinity of operations
  • 7. Equipment Inspected and Assembled — Oxy-arc umbilical, ground G-clamp, oxygen hi-flow regulator, and cutting torch assembled. Equipment confirmed inspected and fit for purpose
  • 8. Regulator Flash Arrester & PRV — Oxygen-rated regulator confirmed to incorporate a flashback arrester AND pressure relief valve on its downstream LP side
  • 9. DC Generator Polarity and Amperage — DC generator set at correct amperage output. Umbilical connected to ensure NEGATIVE supply to cutting torch. Polarity confirmed by bubble test
  • 10. System Function Tested on Deck — Oxy-arc cutting system function tested on deck. Hot work permit conditions satisfied for deck test
  • 11. Electrical Supply Isolated — Electrical supply (knife switch) confirmed OPEN (isolated) prior to subsea deployment
  • 12. Oxygen Line Charged and Isolated — Oxygen line left charged at correct pressure for working depth, then isolated at quad valve, prior to deployment
  • 13. Catenary Support Considered — Consideration given to supporting the burning rig catenary on deployment (D-rings, running shackles, clump weight)
  • 14. Spare Collets and Washers Attached — Spare collets and washers of correct size confirmed attached to the cutting torch assembly
  • 15. Cutting Rods Prepared — Sufficient cutting rods of correct diameter prepared and available for the full subsea operation plus contingency
  • 16. Diver PPE Confirmed — Cutting visor (correct shade), surgical gloves, and rubber-coated hot-water gloves provided and confirmed for the diver

Subsea Operations Checklist — IMCA D 003 Appendix 3

0%
  • 1. Cut Location Identified and Verified — Diver correctly identified and marked the cutting location. Verification confirmed by second diver or ROV independently
  • 2. Gas Entrapment Assessed — Diver/ROV fully assessed worksite for potential gas entrapment in cavities, structural members, or overhead areas that would constitute a risk
  • 3. Vent Holes Identified and Confirmed — Diver positively identified vent hole positions and marked accordingly. Second diver/ROV validated locations. Dive supervisor and client representative confirmed agreement
  • 4. Vent Hole Cutting Method Agreed — Diver and supervisor agreed on whether to cut vent holes with oxy-arc or cold-cutting method
  • 5. Bell Position Confirmed — Dive supervisor considered diving bell position relative to waste gas. Diver's pneumofathometer used to assess gas discharge and plume position relative to bell
  • 6. Cut Section Rigging Assessed — Diver assessed whether cut section requires support rigging prior to severance to prevent injury or damage from falling/moving section
  • 7. Earth/Ground Connection Position Confirmed — Diver identified ground connection position that does not place himself within the electrical circuit between earth and torch
  • 8. Ground Clamp Connected — Bare Metal — Ground/earth G-clamp confirmed connected to redundant steelwork, cleaned to bare metal for good electrical contact
  • 9. Protective Material Installed — Diver considered requirement for protective material in vicinity of cut to prevent inadvertent contact with ignited rod or molten slag
  • 10. Diver Body Position and Life Support Safe — Diver positioned such that no molten slag or cut sections can land on himself or his life support umbilical
  • 11. Commands Confirmed — Divers and topside confirmed conversant with recommended verbal commands ("make it hot" / "make it cold") for cutting operations

Post-Use & Maintenance Checklist

0%
  • 1. DC Generator Off — Dive supervisor confirmed DC generator switched off on completion of cutting operations
  • 2. Oxygen Isolated and Off — Oxygen supply isolated at regulator and turned off at supply quad. Residual O₂ in umbilical vented on recovery
  • 3. Equipment Recovered — Umbilical — Subsea umbilical recovered in reverse of deployment. Equipment cleared from worksite and confirmed clear to surface
  • 4. Torch Disconnected — Cap Installed — Cutting torch disconnected from umbilical. Temporary cap installed on oxygen hose fitting to prevent contamination
  • 5. Torch Washed with Fresh Water — Cutting torch washed with fresh water to remove salt water, slag, and contaminants. Allowed to fully air dry
  • 6. Torch Head Disassembled and Inspected — Torch head disassembled per manufacturer instructions. Inspected for: corrosion, clogging, screen burn-out. Items replaced as necessary
  • 7. Flash Arrestor Inspected — Flash arrestor assembly checked for corrosion (electrolysis damage) and clogging that could reduce oxygen flow. Replaced as complete unit if required
  • 8. O-Rings Inspected — All O-rings inspected for wear, cracking, deformation. O₂-safe silicone grease applied where appropriate. Damaged O-rings replaced
  • 9. Torch Reassembled per Manufacturer Instructions — Torch reassembled following Broco BR-22 Plus operator manual assembly sequence
  • 10. Stored Dry, Oil-Free — Torch and remaining rods stored in a dry, oil-free environment. Oxygen-related equipment separated from any hydrocarbon-contaminated storage areas
  • 11. Defect Report Completed — Any defects, anomalies, or incidents noted and logged. Equipment tagged Out of Service if faults found that cannot be immediately rectified
  • 12. Planned Maintenance Intervals Checked — Confirmed next scheduled maintenance dates for gas cylinders, electrical equipment, and umbilical hose per IMCA D 018 schedules
16
Dos & Don'ts
Critical safety rules organised by hazard category

Oxygen and Equipment

✓ Do
  • Use oxygen-safe silicone grease on all O-rings
  • Use oxygen-safe green Teflon tape on fitting threads
  • Maintain O₂ purity at 99.5% minimum
  • Store torch in a dry, clean, oil-free environment
  • Replace flash arrestor assembly as complete unit
  • Pressure test torch at 9.0 bar[g] before every use
  • Keep all components clean and grease-free
  • Use Broco rods exclusively with the Broco BR-22 torch
  • Pressure test O₂ hose using oxygen — never compressed air
✕ Don't
  • Never use oil or petroleum-based grease on any torch parts
  • Never use standard (non-oxygen-safe) Teflon tape on fittings
  • Never test O₂ hose with vessel compressed air — hydrocarbon risk
  • Never use rods from other manufacturers
  • Never operate torch without flash arrestor and screen in place
  • Never use burning bars (diameter >9.5 mm or length >914 mm)
  • Never disassemble the flash arrestor, coupler hose, or check valve assemblies
  • Never modify equipment from manufacturer specifications

Electrical Safety

✓ Do
  • Use DC power only — always verify with ammeter
  • Confirm electrode is NEGATIVE, ground is POSITIVE
  • Open knife switch before every rod change
  • Keep knife switch within tender reach at all times
  • Wrap all connections with rubber strips + electrical tape (100 mm each side)
  • Use rubber (linesman) electrical insulating gloves at all times
  • Use separate battery/generator — never vessel's primary battery
  • Verify actual amps with clamp ammeter
✕ Don't
  • Never use AC power under any circumstances
  • Never energise torch without diver's direct command
  • Never position diver between ground and cutting rod
  • Never allow diver to turn their back to the ground connection
  • Never work in the splash zone with a live circuit
  • Never touch live electrical parts
  • Never use the vessel's primary battery for cutting
  • Never rely on dial indicators on the welding machine for current

Gas and Explosion Prevention

✓ Do
  • Always pre-purge rod for 4 seconds before calling for power
  • Cut vent holes higher than the cut position before entering enclosed spaces
  • Flood enclosed areas with inert gas (argon/nitrogen) before cutting
  • Drill to equalise pressure differentials before cutting
  • Clear mud from behind the cut before cutting below mudline
  • Check what is behind material being cut before starting
  • Extinguish rod by releasing O₂ lever — not by cutting power
✕ Don't
  • Never energise rod without the 4-second oxygen pre-purge
  • Never cut into enclosed spaces without a vent hole above the cut
  • Never assume there is no gas behind a sealed structure
  • Never cut across a pressure differential without equalising first
  • Never cut below the mudline without clearing mud first
  • Never use thermal cutting methods if explosive gas is suspected
  • Never continue cutting if an oxygen pop moves the diver's hands/arms

Diver Operations

✓ Do
  • Use surface supplied air with hard-wire communications at all times
  • Keep rod tip in direct contact with work ("metal to metal")
  • Pull (drag) the rod — never push it
  • Hold rod like a pool cue — free hand 100 mm from tip
  • Release oxygen lever when rod burns to 3" from collet
  • Have standby diver ready at all times
  • Be aware of environment — falling material, unstable structures
  • Use welding lens shade appropriate for water visibility
✕ Don't
  • Never use SCUBA for underwater cutting operations
  • Never try to maintain an arc gap
  • Never allow hands closer than 100 mm from electrode tip
  • Never contact rod to helmet, hose, cylinder, or life support equipment
  • Never cut without topside tender monitoring operations
  • Never cut without a standby diver available
  • Never continue if an oxygen pop is strong enough to move arms
  • Never cut with damaged rod or damaged torch
17
Troubleshooting
Diagnosing and resolving common operational problems
ℹ️
Most Problems Stem from Wrong Technique

Broco ultrathermic cutting rods differ fundamentally from steel tubular rods. They require higher oxygen flow, lower amperage, and direct contact with the work — not maintenance of an arc. Most operational problems stem from operators applying steel tubular rod setup and technique to ultrathermic rods.

Oxygen Supply Problems

Inadequate Oxygen Flow
Oxygen
Symptoms: Rod burns longer than 60 seconds continuous use; cuts of less than 300 mm in 12 mm steel plate; oxygen jet less than 150 mm from a horizontal rod tip.

Diagnostic steps:
  • A. Ensure a high-volume two-stage oxygen regulator and 9.5 mm ID hose are being used — these are mandatory, not optional
  • B. Ensure the hose is not kinked, pinched, looped, or obstructed anywhere in the run. Examine subsea with ROV if available.
  • C. Ensure the oxygen cylinder valve (all valves if using a manifold) is fully open
  • D. Remove rod — confirm flow rate from bare torch. If flow is restored, the rod bore is blocked — discard rod
  • E. Remove collet and washer — confirm washer is not swollen/deformed and restricting flow
  • F. Recover umbilical to deck — disassemble torch head, inspect for clogging in flash arrester or screen burn-out. Replace as required.
  • G. If umbilical is clear and torch is clear: confirm regulator set pressure is correct for working depth using Table 1/Calculator
Poor Oxygen Purity — Erratic/Intermittent Burning
Oxygen
Symptoms: Rods continuously extinguish underwater, or burn erratically/inconsistently. All flow checks normal.

Cause: Oxygen purity below 99.5% minimum. Broco ultrathermic rods require high-purity O₂ to sustain ignition without power. Each 1% reduction in purity (with continuous power) reduces cutting efficiency by approximately 25%.

Solution: Obtain oxygen from a different, unrelated manufacturer/supplier. Note: changing cylinders filled at the same facility will not solve a purity problem — all cylinders from one facility typically share the same O₂ source.

Electrical / Power Problems

Rod Consumes Too Quickly
Electrical
Symptoms: Rod consumed in significantly less than 45 seconds. Excessive melting rate. Torch head heating rapidly.

Cause: Welding power source delivering greater than 150 amps. Note: dial indicators on welding machines are notoriously inaccurate.

Solution: Use an in-line or clamp-type ammeter to verify actual current output. Reduce output until 150A is confirmed at the rod tip (accounting for cable losses per Table 2).
Cannot Strike Arc — No Ignition
Electrical
Diagnostic checklist (in order):
  • DC generator operating and umbilical connected?
  • Knife switch closed on tender's command? Confirm verbally.
  • Amperage setting appropriate? Verify with tong tester.
  • Polarity correct? Negative to cutting torch? Confirm with bubble test.
  • Earth/ground connection clean and firmly attached? Diver to physically confirm G-clamp is secure and on bare metal.
  • Test with test plate: attach test plate directly to G-clamp and attempt to strike arc on the plate — if successful, the fault is the main ground connection.
  • Cable connections intact? Inspect for broken conductors or corroded lugs.

Torch / Cutting Problems

Oxygen Bubbles from Collet Nut During Cutting
Torch
Action sequence:
  1. Release oxygen lever to extinguish rod. Call for power off.
  2. Remove rod from collet. Visually inspect rod end for damage.
  3. Inspect collet washer for damage, cuts, or deformation.
  4. Reseat rod firmly against collet washer. Tighten collet nut securely.
  5. Squeeze lever briefly to test — if bubbles continue, stop immediately.
  6. If bubbles continue after reseating: torch must be recovered topside and repaired. Do not continue to use.
Note: A loose rod-to-collet connection can cause arcing within the torch and potential flashback — this is a safety-critical fault.
Oxygen Pop — Strong Enough to Move Diver's Arms
Blowback
Immediate action:
  1. STOP WORK IMMEDIATELY. Release oxygen lever.
  2. Call for power off.
  3. Recover torch to surface.
  4. Inspect flash arrestor and screen. Replace if any signs of damage, blockage, or burnout.
  5. Do not resume cutting until flash arrestor has been inspected and replaced if necessary.
Note: Oxygen pops can disintegrate the flash arrester and screen. The flash arrestor is your primary protection against a blowback propagating through the torch.
Poor Cut Quality / Incomplete Penetration
Technique
Symptoms: Rough cut edges, frequent bridges, excessive back-spray, rod consumed without full penetration.

Common causes and corrections:
  • Moving too fast: Slow down — move only when full penetration is confirmed by sound and visual
  • Maintaining arc gap: Keep rod in direct metal-to-metal contact. The ultrathermic process requires contact, not an arc gap
  • Wrong rod angle: Adjust to 30–90 degrees as required. Rod too upright on thin material; too flat on thick material
  • Insufficient O₂ flow: Check oxygen jet is 6"+ horizontal. Verify pressure setting for depth
  • Wrong rod size: Use 9.5 mm rod for material 12 mm and thicker
18
Post-Use Maintenance & Certification
Post-use procedures, cleaning, storage, and IMCA D 018 certification intervals

Immediate Post-Use Procedures

These steps are to be completed immediately upon completion of cutting operations and recovery of equipment to deck. Good preventative maintenance practices ensure less operational downtime.

  1. 1
    Disconnect Torch and Cap Oxygen Hose

    Disconnect cutting torch from umbilical. Immediately install a temporary cap on the oxygen hose fitting to prevent contamination and water ingress.

  2. 2
    Fresh Water Wash and Dry

    Wash cutting torch thoroughly with fresh water. Remove all salt water, slag, and contaminants. Allow to fully dry before reassembly — water in the oxygen system is a contamination risk.

  3. 3
    Torch Head Disassembly and Inspection

    Disassemble torch head per Broco BR-22 Plus manufacturer instructions. Inspect for: corrosion damage (particularly electrolysis on fittings), clogging in oxygen path, screen burnout. Replace damaged items. Reassemble per manufacturer instructions.

  4. 4
    Flash Arrestor Inspection

    Inspect flash arrestor for corrosion, electrolysis damage, and clogging that could reduce oxygen flow. If any damage is found, replace the flash arrestor assembly as a complete unit. Never attempt to clean a clogged arrestor — replace it.

  5. 5
    Store Correctly

    Store cutting torch and remaining rods in a dry, oil-free environment. Never store in areas where hydrocarbon contamination could occur. Never store oxygen equipment near petroleum products.

IMCA D 018 Certification Intervals

Source: IMCA D 003 Rev.2, Section 6 and Appendices 4–6. Reference: IMCA D 018 Code of Practice on the Initial and Periodic Examination, Testing and Certification of Diving Plant and Equipment.

Equipment CategoryExamination / TestValidity PeriodCompetent Person Category
Gas Cylinders & Pressure Vessels (Detail Sheet 9.1)External visual examination6 monthsCat 1, 2, 3 or 4
Thorough external visual + gas leak test to max working pressure2.5 yearsCat 3 or 4
Thorough internal & external visual + leak test to max working pressure (+ NDE if required)5 yearsCat 3 or 4
Electrical Equipment (Detail Sheet 11)Visual examination + function test (incl. protective devices) + continuity and resistance tests of all cables6 monthsCat 2, 3 or 4
Visual + function test + continuity/resistance per detailed electrical testing scheduleOngoingCat 2, 3 or 4
Umbilical Hose Components (Detail Sheet 28)Visual examination and function test6 monthsCat 1, 2, 3 or 4
Pressure leak test to maximum rated working pressure2 yearsCat 1, 2, 3 or 4

Note: If an umbilical hose has an end fitting changed or replaced, the hose requires retesting under 'when new' category (hydraulic overpressure test at 1.5× max rated working pressure).

Cleaning: All oxy-arc equipment must be cleaned to oxygen service standards per IMCA D 031 — Cleaning for Oxygen Service: Setting up Facilities and Procedures.

19
FMECA — Principal Failure Modes
Failure Mode, Effect and Criticality Analysis — IMCA D 003 §3.5 (ref. HSE OTH 349)

The following five principal failure modes (PFMs) have been identified by formal FMECA analysis for oxy-arc cutting operations. Each must be addressed in the pre-job risk assessment and mitigated by specific control measures.

PFM 1 — Blowback
Critical
Cause: Spontaneous combustion of trapped oxygen at the cutting point
Effect: Explosive pressure wave transmitted back through cutting torch — potential injury to diver, damage to equipment, torch destruction
Controls: Functional flash arrestor in place and maintained; periodic inspection on PMI programme; good diver technique minimises frequency; stop work if blowback strong enough to move diver's arms
PFM 2 — Blowback
Critical
Cause: Steam explosion — rapid boiling of water trapped in enclosed space, heated by cutting process
Effect: Sudden violent pressure release; potential structural damage; diver injury from pressure wave or debris
Controls: Vent holes cut before entering enclosed spaces; verify no trapped water in sealed tubulars; assess for steam generation potential in risk assessment
PFM 3 — Loss of Power
High
Cause: Poor electrical connection — corroded lugs, loose connections, damaged cable
Effect: Inability to ignite rod; accumulated oxygen build-up during multiple failed ignition attempts; potential for sudden gas explosion
Controls: Pre-dive electrical inspection; UL-approved tape on all connections; ground clamp on bare metal; polarity test before diving; diagnose systematically per Section 17
PFM 4 — Hose / Umbilical Failure
High
Cause: Failure of couplings; abrasion damage; crimp over sharp edges
Effect: Loss of oxygen supply (cutting stops); hose whip (high pressure hose under tension snapping — line whip injury risk); fire risk from O₂ release near heat source
Controls: Umbilical treated as diver umbilical — same respect and care; D-rings support weight; no deployment over sharp edges; 2-year pressure test cycle; visual inspection before each deployment
PFM 5 — Failure to Strike Arc
Medium
Cause: Poor ground conditions — insufficient electrical contact between ground clamp and conductive workpiece
Effect: Repeated failed arc-strike attempts with oxygen flowing — accumulation of oxygen in work area; potential ignition source risk; diver frustration leading to unsafe improvisation
Controls: Clean ground connection to bare metal before deployment; confirm G-clamp physically firm; test with test plate on G-clamp if arc fails; systematic diagnostic per Section 17
20
Questions & Answers
Frequently asked questions on Broco ultrathermic cutting operations

Click any question to expand the answer. All answers are sourced directly from Broco Inc. operating instructions and IMCA D 003 Rev.2.

Q1: Can ultrathermic rods continue to burn after the electrical power is switched off? ▼

Yes. Broco Underwater Ultrathermic cutting rods will continue to burn as long as the oxygen flow is maintained, even with the power completely off. This is the fundamental characteristic of the exothermic (ultrathermic) process — once the rod ignites, the internal exothermic chemical reaction sustains combustion independently of electrical power.

The ONLY way to extinguish a burning ultrathermic rod is to release the oxygen lever. Switching off the welding machine will not stop the rod from burning.

Source: Broco Inc. Operating Instructions, Underwater Operations Note; IMCA D 003 Rev.2 §4.1

Q2: Why must I never use AC power for underwater cutting? ▼

Using alternating current (AC) for underwater cutting can electrocute the diver. Water is an excellent conductor of electricity. AC current at typical welding voltages is lethal in an underwater environment because it alternates direction rapidly, preventing the body's natural resistance from limiting current flow as effectively as with DC.

Only direct current (DC) from a known, verified power source may be used. The polarity must also be correct: electrode negative (−), ground positive (+).

Source: Broco Inc. Operating Instructions, Topside Warning; IMCA D 003 Rev.2 §4.1

Q3: Why must I pre-purge the cutting rod for 4 seconds before calling for power? ▼

Explosive gases can accumulate within an energised underwater cutting rod if oxygen flow is not established first. The 4-second pre-purge before calling for power serves two purposes:

  • Establishes full oxygen flow through the rod before any arc is struck, ensuring the exothermic reaction begins cleanly rather than in a potentially explosive gas mixture
  • Purges any water or gas from the rod bore that might cause a blowback when the arc is struck

Do NOT energise a cutting rod until ready to initiate cutting, and always pre-purge for a minimum of 4 seconds first.

Source: Broco Inc. Operating Instructions, Underwater Operations Warning

Q4: How do I calculate the correct oxygen regulator pressure for my working depth? ▼

The metric working formula is: Gauge Pressure (bar[g]) = 6.21 + (0.101 × depth in metres) + (0.0226 × hose length in metres)

  • Base pressure needed at rod tip: 6.21 bar[g]
  • Depth correction: add 0.101 bar for every metre of working depth
  • Hose friction correction: add 0.0226 bar for every metre of oxygen supply hose

Example: Working at 30m with 90m of hose: 6.21 + (0.101 × 30) + (0.0226 × 90) = 6.21 + 3.03 + 2.03 = 11.27 bar[g] at the regulator.

Use the interactive calculator in Section 11 for real-time calculations.

Source: Broco Inc. Table 1 footnote; IMCA D 003 Rev.2 §4.3 Note

Q5: What do I do if oxygen bubbles escape from the collet nut during cutting? ▼

Oxygen bubbles escaping from the collet nut indicate a loose seal between the rod and the collet washer. This is a safety issue — a loose connection can cause electrical arcing within the torch and potential flashback.

Procedure:

  1. Release oxygen lever — stop cutting
  2. Remove the rod from the collet
  3. Reseat the rod firmly against the collet washer, ensuring it is fully bottomed out
  4. Tighten the collet nut securely
  5. Test again briefly — if bubbles continue, stop using the torch immediately and have it repaired

Source: Broco Inc. Operating Instructions, Underwater Operations

Q6: Why should I not use a "burning bar" for underwater cutting? ▼

A burning bar is any underwater cutting rod operating with oxygen as a fuel source whose diameter is greater than 9.5 mm OR length is longer than 914 mm. Burning bars cause excessive gas build-up and can trigger an oxygen explosion. Their large bore produces a far greater volume of free oxygen than a standard cutting rod, and this oxygen accumulates in the work area at a rate that cannot be safely managed.

The Broco BR-22 Plus system uses rods up to 9.5 mm diameter and up to 914 mm length specifically to stay within the safe working parameters of the ultrathermic cutting system.

Source: Broco Inc. Operating Instructions, Cutting Equipment Warnings

Q7: Why must I never use SCUBA for underwater cutting operations? ▼

There are two critical reasons why SCUBA is prohibited for underwater cutting:

  • Electrocution risk: A SCUBA diver operating in a live electrical circuit has no insulation between their breathing apparatus (which contacts their face) and the water. The electrical current can travel through the water to the diver's body via the regulator/mouthpiece — with potentially fatal results
  • No communications: Underwater cutting requires continuous two-way hard-wire communication with the topside tender. SCUBA does not provide this. The tender must be able to immediately open/close the knife switch on diver command — without communications, this is impossible

Always use surface supplied breathing air and hard-wire communications. Life support equipment must not be modified from manufacturer's original specifications.

Source: Broco Inc. Operating Instructions, Underwater Operations Warnings; IMCA D 003 Rev.2 §4.1

Q8: How do I cut concrete or rock with the Broco system? ▼

Concrete and rock are non-conductive materials — they cannot be cut by the standard arc-ignition method since no electrical circuit can be completed at the workpiece. The procedure uses a striker plate:

  1. Always use the 9.5 mm diameter rod only for concrete/rock
  2. Attach a copper or steel plate (striker plate) to the ground cable
  3. Strike the rod against the striker plate to ignite the rod with oxygen flowing and power on
  4. Once ignited, keep oxygen lever depressed and raise rod from plate — call for current off
  5. Press burning rod into concrete/rock to create a molten pool
  6. Move rod in, out, and around to enlarge hole and vent gases — critical to prevent gas build-up
  7. Ensure entry hole is large enough to vent all gases during the melt

Source: Broco Inc. Operating Instructions, Techniques — Concrete and Rock

Q9: What does an oxygen pop mean and how should I respond? ▼

An oxygen pop is a spontaneous explosion of varying intensity at the cutting point, caused by hydrogen formation or spontaneous combustion of trapped oxygen. Most are minor — caused by small pockets of hydrogen or oxygen built up at the work face — and result in minimal discomfort. They are addressed as a guideword in the IMCA risk assessment process (blowback).

If the oxygen pop is strong enough to move the diver's hands or arms:

  1. STOP WORK IMMEDIATELY
  2. Release oxygen lever to extinguish rod
  3. Call for power off
  4. Recover torch to surface
  5. Inspect flash arrester and screen thoroughly — replace if any damage found
  6. Do not resume until fault is investigated and cleared

Good cutting technique and correct diver positioning reduce the frequency and severity of oxygen pops. The flash arrestor is the primary safety device protecting against pops propagating back into the torch.

Source: Broco Inc. Operating Instructions, Underwater Operations; IMCA D 003 Rev.2 Guideword: Blowback

Q10: Why is oxy-arc cutting more dangerous in the splash zone? ▼

The splash zone — the area where the diver is only partially immersed in water — is identified as the most hazardous location for underwater cutting operations. When a diver is only partially immersed with a live electrical circuit:

  • The path of least electrical resistance through the water changes unpredictably as waves cover and uncover the diver's body
  • Air has much higher resistance than water — as body parts become exposed to air and then re-submerged, the electrical circuit changes dramatically
  • The risk of severe electrical shock is significantly greater than either fully immersed or fully out of water

IMCA D 003 states oxy-arc cutting in the splash zone should be avoided. If operations near the waterline are unavoidable, full wet or dry suit, rubber insulating gloves, eye protection, and a dive helmet are mandatory.

Source: IMCA D 003 Rev.2 §4.1; Broco Inc. Operating Instructions, Diver Safety Warning

Q11: How do I know if I have adequate oxygen flow before cutting? ▼

There is a simple and reliable visual test: hold the cutting torch horizontally and fully depress the oxygen lever. A visible gas jet should extend from the cutting rod tip.

  • Subsea minimum: 150 mm oxygen jet from rod tip
  • IMCA standard: Minimum 300 mm gas jet length

If the jet is less than 150 mm, do not attempt to cut. Investigate the oxygen supply path — regulator pressure, hose restrictions, collet/washer blockage, or flash arrestor clogging — before proceeding.

Source: Broco Inc. Operating Instructions, Troubleshooting; IMCA D 003 Rev.2 §5.2

Q12: What is the maximum depth for battery-powered operations? ▼

Use of a battery power source (12V or 24V DC vehicle/boat battery) is not recommended for cutting at depths below 60 fsw (18m). Beyond this depth, battery voltage may be insufficient to reliably ignite the rod, resulting in repeated failed ignition attempts with oxygen continuously flowing — which creates an accumulating oxygen gas hazard at the worksite.

At shallow depths (≤60 fsw), a vehicle or boat battery (12V or 24V DC) is a practical alternative to a welding machine. A safety knife switch is still recommended even with battery power. The tender must disconnect the battery immediately on the diver's command once the rod is ignited — to preserve battery life.

Source: Broco Inc. Operating Instructions, Techniques — Battery Operations

Q13: What are the implications of oxygen purity being below 99.5%? ▼

Oxygen purity below 99.5% has significant operational impacts:

  • The rod requires oxygen of at least 99.5% purity to sustain ignition without continuous power
  • When cutting with continuous power, a reduction in oxygen purity of just 1% decreases cutting efficiency by approximately 25%
  • Symptoms of poor oxygen purity: rods continuously extinguish underwater, or burn erratically and inconsistently

Solution: Source oxygen from a different, unrelated manufacturer. Note that changing cylinders from the same supplier at the same facility will typically not solve the problem, as all their cylinders share the same oxygen source.

Source: Broco Inc. Operating Instructions, Troubleshooting — Power Source Problems

Q14: Why should the oxy-arc cutting umbilical be treated like a diver's umbilical? ▼

The oxy-arc cutting umbilical carries three critical supplies simultaneously: the oxygen hose (high-pressure oxygen under tension), the electrical power cable (live DC circuit during cutting), and the electrical earth/ground cable. Damage to any of these components can cause:

  • Oxygen hose failure: Loss of cutting, hose whip injury, fire risk from high-pressure O₂ release
  • Power cable damage: Loss of ignition; short-circuit risk; electrocution potential if insulation fails
  • Ground cable damage: Loss of electrical circuit; inability to ignite rod; arc strike failure

The umbilical must be handled carefully: D-rings at 15m intervals, no deployment over sharp edges, no crimping or kinking of the O₂ hose, secured on deck without tension. It should be included in the pre-dive inspection and the IMCA D 018 maintenance schedule.

Source: IMCA D 003 Rev.2 §4.4

Q15: What surface practice is recommended for personnel new to oxy-arc cutting? ▼

New operators should complete surface practice before any underwater attempt:

  1. Remove tape from rods before surface burning (fume risk)
  2. Burn at least 3–4 rods on the surface before attempting underwater cutting
  3. Practice metal-to-metal contact technique — not arc maintenance
  4. Practice keeping the rod tip in the molten puddle
  5. Adjust travel speed until full penetration is consistently achieved

A diver with surface arc welding or cutting qualifications will achieve excellent results underwater in a short time. The key adjustment is the transition from "holding an arc" (welding mindset) to "direct contact in the puddle" (ultrathermic mindset).

IMCA D 003 also emphasises addressing skill fade — due to the infrequent use of oxy-arc cutting, training and assessment of key personnel (divers, supervisors, project engineers) under safe, controlled conditions prior to commencing offshore works is strongly recommended.

Source: Broco Inc. Operating Instructions, Surface Practice for New Users; IMCA D 003 Rev.2 §3.3.2

Q16: What is the correct procedure if the valve stem on the BR-22 Plus gets stuck? ▼

The BR-22 Plus incorporates a redesigned valve lever that allows manual return in the event that the valve stem gets stuck — this is a specific improvement over the previous BR-22 model.

The valve lever can be manually repositioned to return the stuck stem to the closed position. This gives the diver direct mechanical control of the oxygen flow even when the internal valve mechanism fails to spring-return automatically.

Additionally, the control valve has been redesigned to shut off oxygen flow even with complete O-ring seal failure — the valve seat provides a positive shutoff independent of the O-ring condition. This is a critical safety feature for maintaining diver control of oxygen in emergency conditions.

Source: Broco Inc. BR-22 Plus Improved Features, Items 7 and 8

Q17: When should I select a 6 mm rod versus a 9.5 mm rod? ▼

The selection depends on material thickness, conditions, and task requirement:

ScenarioRod SizeReason
Steel up to 12 mm — maximum economy6 mmNarrow kerf, fine cutting, economical per cut length
Steel 12 mm and thicker9.5 mmBetter penetration, higher production rate
Bad visibility9.5 mmWider kerf easier to locate; more forgiving technique
Concrete, rock, coral9.5 mm onlyMandatory per Broco instructions
Wire rope — fine cut control6 mmBetter precision; requires continuous metal contact
Rope, wood (general)9.5 mmMore power, easier to maintain contact
Non-ferrous metals over 75 mm9.5 mmIncrease O₂ to 7.6 bar[g] over ambient

Source: Broco Inc. Operating Instructions, Techniques section

Q18: What is the correct competence requirement for oxy-arc cutting operations? ▼

IMCA D 003 Rev.2 §7 — Competence states:

  • Oxy-arc cutting requires a specific skill set — it is not a general commercial diving competence
  • The dive supervisor must satisfy himself that the task is being conducted by suitably trained and competent personnel
  • The person carrying out the task must also be personally confident in their own ability prior to performing the task
  • Personnel must refuse to perform the task if not confident they can conduct it safely — this is an explicit right and responsibility
  • It is the employing company's responsibility to ensure personnel selection for oxy-arc cutting is captured within the company competence scheme
  • Skill fade from infrequent use must be addressed — training and assessment prior to each project is recommended

Additionally, Broco requires that employers provide operator training before allowing personnel to use the system.

Source: IMCA D 003 Rev.2 §7; Broco Inc. Operating Instructions, General Warning

21
Knowledge Assessment Quiz
15 questions — Pass mark 80% (12/15) — Completion record submitted to training supervisors

Instructions: Answer all 15 questions. All questions are drawn directly from this manual and IMCA D 003 / Broco BR-22 Plus documentation. On submission, your result will be displayed and a completion report prepared for dispatch to F. Schrijver and M. Jeffery. Pass mark: 80% (12 out of 15).

Answered: 0 / 15 Status: Not submitted
Q01 · REF: Broco OCI §Underwater Operations / IMCA D 003 §4.1
What is the ONLY way to extinguish a burning Broco ultrathermic cutting rod?
✓ Correct answer: Release the oxygen lever. Broco ultrathermic rods continue to burn as long as oxygen flow is maintained — even with power completely off. Switching off the welding machine will NOT stop combustion. Only releasing the oxygen lever extinguishes the rod. (Broco OCI Underwater Operations Note; IMCA D 003 §4.1)
Q02 · REF: IMCA D 003 §4.1 / Broco OCI Topside Warning
What type of electrical current and polarity is required for underwater oxy-arc cutting?
✓ Correct answer: DC, electrode negative (−). AC current will kill the diver. Only DC from a known, verified source is permitted. Polarity: cutting torch/electrode = negative (−); earth/ground clamp = positive (+). (IMCA D 003 §4.1)
Q03 · REF: Broco BR-22 Plus Table 1 / IMCA D 003 §4.3
What is the base oxygen pressure required at the rod tip (before depth and hose corrections)?
✓ Correct answer: 6.21 bar[g]. Formula: Regulator pressure = 6.21 + (0.101 × depth in m) + (0.0226 × hose length in m). The base 6.21 bar[g] is the minimum required at the rod tip regardless of depth. (Broco BR-22 Plus Table 1)
Q04 · REF: Broco OCI Underwater Operations Warning
What is the minimum oxygen pre-purge time required before calling for electrical power ("make it hot")?
✓ Correct answer: 4 seconds. The pre-purge establishes full oxygen flow before any arc is struck, purges water/gas from the rod bore, and prevents potential blowback. Never energise a rod until ready to cut — always pre-purge for minimum 4 seconds. (Broco OCI Underwater Operations Warning)
Q05 · REF: IMCA D 003 §4.2 / Broco OCI Operating Procedure
What is the correct verbal command the diver uses to request electrical power from the topside tender?
✓ Correct answer: "Make it hot." The topside tender closes the knife switch only on the command "make it hot". The command "make it cold" is used to request isolation. These verbal commands must be confirmed and rehearsed before commencing operations. (IMCA D 003 §4.2)
Q06 · REF: Broco OCI Techniques / IMCA D 003
Which rod diameter is mandatory (no alternative permitted) when cutting concrete, rock, or coral?
✓ Correct answer: 9.5 mm — mandatory. For concrete, rock and coral, the 9.5 mm rod is the only permitted rod size. Power is switched off after ignition for non-conductive materials (striker plate used for ignition). (Broco OCI Techniques)
Q07 · REF: Broco OCI Cutting Equipment Warnings
A "burning bar" is prohibited because it exceeds which parameters?
✓ Correct answer: Diameter >9.5 mm OR length >914 mm. Burning bars cause excessive free oxygen accumulation which can trigger an oxygen explosion. The BR-22 Plus system uses rods specifically dimensioned to stay within safe parameters. (Broco OCI Cutting Equipment Warnings)
Q08 · REF: Broco OCI Warnings / IMCA D 003 §4.1
Why is SCUBA diving equipment prohibited for all underwater cutting operations?
✓ Correct answer: Both electrocution risk AND no hard-wire communications. Current can travel through water to the diver's body via the regulator. Without hard-wire comms, the tender cannot open/close the knife switch on command. Both factors are independently fatal. (IMCA D 003 §4.1)
Q09 · REF: IMCA D 003 Appendix 3 — Subsea Checklist
Where must the earth/ground G-clamp be connected for underwater cutting?
✓ Correct answer: Redundant steelwork, bare metal, outside the diver's circuit path. The diver must not be positioned within the electrical circuit between earth clamp and torch. The clamp must be cleaned to bare metal for good electrical contact. (IMCA D 003 Appendix 3)
Q10 · REF: Broco OCI Operating Technique
What is the correct rod-to-work angle range during underwater cutting operations?
✓ Correct answer: 30 to 90 degrees depending on material thickness. Unlike arc welding, the rod tip must be kept in direct contact with the work — do NOT hold an arc gap. Hold the rod approximately 100 mm from the tip for stability. (Broco OCI Operating Technique)
Q11 · REF: IMCA D 003 §4.1 / Broco OCI Topside Procedures
When must the electrical supply knife switch be in the OPEN (isolated) position?
✓ Correct answer: At all times — closed ONLY on command "make it hot". The knife switch is the primary safety device. It must be OPEN by default and only closed for the specific duration of a cutting arc being struck. Immediately opened again on "make it cold". (IMCA D 003 §4.1)
Q12 · REF: Broco OCI Techniques — Non-Ferrous Metals
What oxygen pressure is specified for cutting non-ferrous metals over 75 mm thick?
✓ Correct answer: 7.58 bar[g] over ambient. Broco instructions specify increasing oxygen pressure for non-ferrous metal sections exceeding 75 mm thickness. Non-ferrous materials do not oxidise like mild steel, requiring more energy. (Broco OCI Techniques)
Q13 · REF: Section 05 — Polarity Test Procedure
In the salt water polarity test, what observation confirms correct polarity (DC electrode negative)?
✓ Correct answer: Heavy stream of bubbles from the cutting rod tip. This confirms DC straight polarity — electrode negative (−). If bubbles rise from the ground plate instead, polarity is reversed and must be corrected before any diving. (Section 05, Polarity Test Procedure)
Q14 · REF: IMCA D 003 §4.3 / Section 04 — Gas Explosion Risks
What must be done BEFORE commencing the main cut on any enclosed structural member (tubular, caisson, box section)?
✓ Correct answer: Cut a vent hole above and behind the planned cut position. The vent must be confirmed open and flowing before the main cut begins. If explosive gas contamination is suspected, the vent hole must be cold-cut first. (IMCA D 003 §4.3)
Q15 · REF: IMCA D 003 §7 — Competence
What does IMCA D 003 §7 specify regarding a diver's right and responsibility if not confident to perform an oxy-arc cutting task?
✓ Correct answer: Personnel must refuse — explicit right and responsibility. IMCA D 003 §7 states the person carrying out the task must be personally confident in their own ability. Refusal is not only permitted but required if confidence is absent. (IMCA D 003 §7)

Answer all 15 questions before submitting. Once submitted, answers cannot be changed.
